title: Exercises: Multipartite Entanglement
description: Practice constructing and analyzing multipartite entangled states including GHZ, W, and graph states

exercises.title: GHZ vs W State Properties
exercises.description: Implement functions to create n-qubit GHZ and W states, then analyze their behavior under qubit loss. Compute the reduced density matrix when one qubit is traced out and determine whether entanglement is preserved.
exercises.starterCode:
import numpy as np

def ghz_state(n):
    """
    Create n-qubit GHZ state: (|00...0> + |11...1>) / sqrt(2)
    
    Args:
        n: number of qubits
    
    Returns:
        State vector of dimension 2^n
    """
    # Your code here
    pass

def w_state(n):
    """
    Create n-qubit W state: sum of all single-excitation states / sqrt(n)
    
    Args:
        n: number of qubits
    
    Returns:
        State vector of dimension 2^n
    """
    # Your code here
    pass

def trace_out_last_qubit(state, n):
    """
    Trace out the last qubit from an n-qubit pure state.
    
    Args:
        state: state vector of dimension 2^n
        n: number of qubits
    
    Returns:
        Reduced density matrix of dimension 2^(n-1) x 2^(n-1)
    """
    # Your code here
    pass

def check_entanglement(rho):
    """
    Check if a 2-qubit density matrix is entangled using PPT criterion.
    
    Returns:
        dict with 'is_entangled', 'negativity'
    """
    # Your code here
    pass

# Test your implementation
print("Analyzing qubit loss in 3-qubit states:")
print("=" * 50)

# GHZ state
ghz3 = ghz_state(3)
rho_ghz_reduced = trace_out_last_qubit(ghz3, 3)
result_ghz = check_entanglement(rho_ghz_reduced)
print(f"GHZ after qubit loss: entangled = {result_ghz['is_entangled']}")

# W state
w3 = w_state(3)
rho_w_reduced = trace_out_last_qubit(w3, 3)
result_w = check_entanglement(rho_w_reduced)
print(f"W after qubit loss: entangled = {result_w['is_entangled']}")
exercises.solution:
import numpy as np

def ghz_state(n):
    """
    Create n-qubit GHZ state: (|00...0> + |11...1>) / sqrt(2)
    """
    dim = 2**n
    state = np.zeros(dim, dtype=complex)
    state[0] = 1 / np.sqrt(2)      # |00...0>
    state[dim - 1] = 1 / np.sqrt(2)  # |11...1>
    return state

def w_state(n):
    """
    Create n-qubit W state: sum of all single-excitation states / sqrt(n)
    """
    dim = 2**n
    state = np.zeros(dim, dtype=complex)
    
    # Add each single-excitation state
    for i in range(n):
        # Index of state with bit i set to 1
        index = 2**(n - 1 - i)
        state[index] = 1 / np.sqrt(n)
    
    return state

def trace_out_last_qubit(state, n):
    """
    Trace out the last qubit from an n-qubit pure state.
    """
    # Full density matrix
    rho = np.outer(state, state.conj())
    
    # Dimensions
    dim_keep = 2**(n - 1)
    dim_trace = 2
    
    # Reshape to separate last qubit
    rho_reshaped = rho.reshape(dim_keep, dim_trace, dim_keep, dim_trace)
    
    # Trace over last qubit (axes 1 and 3)
    rho_reduced = np.trace(rho_reshaped, axis1=1, axis2=3)
    
    return rho_reduced

def check_entanglement(rho):
    """
    Check if a 2-qubit density matrix is entangled using PPT criterion.
    """
    # Assume 2x2 bipartition (4x4 matrix)
    dim = int(np.sqrt(rho.shape[0]))
    
    # Partial transpose
    rho_tensor = rho.reshape(dim, dim, dim, dim)
    rho_pt = np.transpose(rho_tensor, (0, 3, 2, 1)).reshape(dim**2, dim**2)
    
    # Get eigenvalues
    eigenvalues = np.linalg.eigvalsh(rho_pt)
    
    # Negativity
    negative_eigs = eigenvalues[eigenvalues < -1e-10]
    negativity = np.sum(np.abs(negative_eigs))
    
    return {
        'is_entangled': negativity > 1e-10,
        'negativity': negativity,
        'pt_eigenvalues': eigenvalues
    }

# Test implementation
print("Analyzing qubit loss in 3-qubit states:")
print("=" * 50)

# GHZ state
ghz3 = ghz_state(3)
print(f"\n3-qubit GHZ state created")
print(f"Non-zero indices: 0 (|000>) and 7 (|111>)")

rho_ghz_reduced = trace_out_last_qubit(ghz3, 3)
result_ghz = check_entanglement(rho_ghz_reduced)
print(f"\nGHZ after qubit loss:")
print(f"  Reduced density matrix diagonal: {np.diag(rho_ghz_reduced).real}")
print(f"  Entangled: {result_ghz['is_entangled']}")
print(f"  Negativity: {result_ghz['negativity']:.4f}")
print(f"  PT eigenvalues: {result_ghz['pt_eigenvalues']}")

# W state
w3 = w_state(3)
print(f"\n3-qubit W state created")

rho_w_reduced = trace_out_last_qubit(w3, 3)
result_w = check_entanglement(rho_w_reduced)
print(f"\nW after qubit loss:")
print(f"  Entangled: {result_w['is_entangled']}")
print(f"  Negativity: {result_w['negativity']:.4f}")
print(f"  PT eigenvalues: {np.round(result_w['pt_eigenvalues'], 4)}")

print("\n" + "=" * 50)
print("CONCLUSION:")
print("  GHZ: Entanglement destroyed after qubit loss")
print("  W: Entanglement preserved after qubit loss")
hints: GHZ state has only two non-zero amplitudes at indices 0 and 2^n - 1
W state has n non-zero amplitudes at indices 2^(n-1-i) for i = 0 to n-1
Use np.outer to create the density matrix from the state vector
Reshape and use np.trace to perform partial trace

testCases.input: 3-qubit GHZ state, trace out one qubit
3-qubit W state, trace out one qubit

exercises.title: Constructing Graph States
exercises.description: Implement a function to construct graph states from an adjacency matrix. A graph state is created by initializing all qubits in |+> and applying CZ gates for each edge. Test with linear, star, and ring graphs.
exercises.starterCode:
import numpy as np

def create_graph_state(adjacency_matrix):
    """
    Create a graph state from an adjacency matrix.
    
    Steps:
    1. Initialize all qubits in |+> = (|0> + |1>) / sqrt(2)
    2. Apply CZ gate for each edge (i, j) where adj[i,j] = 1
    
    Args:
        adjacency_matrix: n x n symmetric matrix (0s and 1s)
    
    Returns:
        State vector of dimension 2^n
    """
    # Your code here
    pass

def apply_cz(state, qubit_i, qubit_j, n_qubits):
    """
    Apply CZ gate between qubit_i and qubit_j.
    CZ adds a phase of -1 when both qubits are |1>.
    
    Args:
        state: current state vector
        qubit_i, qubit_j: qubit indices (0-indexed)
        n_qubits: total number of qubits
    
    Returns:
        New state vector after CZ
    """
    # Your code here
    pass

def visualize_graph_state(adj, state):
    """
    Print the non-zero amplitudes of a graph state.
    """
    n = len(adj)
    print(f"Graph state ({n} qubits):")
    print(f"Adjacency matrix:\n{adj}")
    print(f"Non-zero amplitudes:")
    for idx, amp in enumerate(state):
        if np.abs(amp) > 1e-10:
            bits = format(idx, f'0{n}b')
            print(f"  |{bits}>: {amp.real:+.4f}")

# Test with different graphs
print("Graph State Construction:")
print("=" * 50)

# Linear graph: 0 -- 1 -- 2
adj_linear = np.array([
    [0, 1, 0],
    [1, 0, 1],
    [0, 1, 0]
])
state_linear = create_graph_state(adj_linear)
visualize_graph_state(adj_linear, state_linear)

# Star graph: 1, 2 connected to center 0
print()
adj_star = np.array([
    [0, 1, 1],
    [1, 0, 0],
    [1, 0, 0]
])
state_star = create_graph_state(adj_star)
visualize_graph_state(adj_star, state_star)

# Ring graph: 0 -- 1 -- 2 -- 0
print()
adj_ring = np.array([
    [0, 1, 1],
    [1, 0, 1],
    [1, 1, 0]
])
state_ring = create_graph_state(adj_ring)
visualize_graph_state(adj_ring, state_ring)
exercises.solution:
import numpy as np

def create_graph_state(adjacency_matrix):
    """
    Create a graph state from an adjacency matrix.
    """
    n = len(adjacency_matrix)
    dim = 2**n
    
    # Step 1: Initialize all qubits in |+>
    # |+>^n = sum over all computational basis states / sqrt(2^n)
    state = np.ones(dim, dtype=complex) / np.sqrt(dim)
    
    # Step 2: Apply CZ for each edge
    for i in range(n):
        for j in range(i + 1, n):
            if adjacency_matrix[i, j] == 1:
                state = apply_cz(state, i, j, n)
    
    return state

def apply_cz(state, qubit_i, qubit_j, n_qubits):
    """
    Apply CZ gate between qubit_i and qubit_j.
    """
    dim = len(state)
    new_state = state.copy()
    
    for idx in range(dim):
        # Convert index to binary representation
        # Check if both qubit_i and qubit_j are in state |1>
        bit_i = (idx >> (n_qubits - 1 - qubit_i)) & 1
        bit_j = (idx >> (n_qubits - 1 - qubit_j)) & 1
        
        if bit_i == 1 and bit_j == 1:
            new_state[idx] *= -1
    
    return new_state

def visualize_graph_state(adj, state):
    """
    Print the non-zero amplitudes of a graph state.
    """
    n = len(adj)
    print(f"Graph state ({n} qubits):")
    print(f"Adjacency matrix:")
    print(adj)
    print(f"Non-zero amplitudes:")
    for idx, amp in enumerate(state):
        if np.abs(amp) > 1e-10:
            bits = format(idx, f'0{n}b')
            print(f"  |{bits}>: {amp.real:+.4f}")

# Test with different graphs
print("Graph State Construction:")
print("=" * 50)

# Linear graph: 0 -- 1 -- 2
print("\nLinear graph (path): 0 -- 1 -- 2")
adj_linear = np.array([
    [0, 1, 0],
    [1, 0, 1],
    [0, 1, 0]
])
state_linear = create_graph_state(adj_linear)
visualize_graph_state(adj_linear, state_linear)

# Verify normalization
print(f"Normalization: {np.linalg.norm(state_linear):.4f}")

# Star graph: 1, 2 connected to center 0
print("\n" + "=" * 50)
print("\nStar graph: center 0, leaves 1 and 2")
adj_star = np.array([
    [0, 1, 1],
    [1, 0, 0],
    [1, 0, 0]
])
state_star = create_graph_state(adj_star)
visualize_graph_state(adj_star, state_star)
print(f"Normalization: {np.linalg.norm(state_star):.4f}")

# Ring graph: 0 -- 1 -- 2 -- 0
print("\n" + "=" * 50)
print("\nRing graph: 0 -- 1 -- 2 -- 0")
adj_ring = np.array([
    [0, 1, 1],
    [1, 0, 1],
    [1, 1, 0]
])
state_ring = create_graph_state(adj_ring)
visualize_graph_state(adj_ring, state_ring)
print(f"Normalization: {np.linalg.norm(state_ring):.4f}")

# Compare structure
print("\n" + "=" * 50)
print("\nObservation:")
print("- All graph states have 2^n non-zero amplitudes")
print("- Amplitudes are all +/- 1/sqrt(2^n)")
print("- The sign pattern encodes the graph structure")
hints: Start with |+>^n which is a uniform superposition of all 2^n basis states
CZ gate multiplies amplitude by -1 only when both qubits are |1>
Use bit manipulation to check qubit values: (idx >> (n-1-qubit)) & 1
Apply CZ gates only for i < j to avoid double-counting edges

testCases.expected: 8 non-zero amplitudes with specific sign pattern

exercises.title: SLOCC Classification via Three-Tangle
exercises.description: Implement the three-tangle calculation to classify three-qubit states into GHZ-class (tau > 0) or W-class (tau = 0). The three-tangle uses the Cayley hyperdeterminant of the coefficient tensor.
exercises.starterCode:
import numpy as np

def three_tangle(state):
    """
    Calculate the three-tangle for a three-qubit state.
    
    The three-tangle tau is calculated from the hyperdeterminant:
    For state coefficients a_{ijk}, tau = 4 |Det(A)|^2
    
    Args:
        state: 8-dimensional state vector
    
    Returns:
        Three-tangle value (0 for W-class, > 0 for GHZ-class)
    """
    # Your code here
    pass

def classify_state(state):
    """
    Classify a three-qubit state based on three-tangle.
    
    Returns:
        'GHZ-class', 'W-class', or 'Product/Biseparable'
    """
    # Your code here
    pass

# Test classification
print("SLOCC Classification using Three-Tangle:")
print("=" * 50)

# GHZ state
ghz = np.zeros(8, dtype=complex)
ghz[0] = ghz[7] = 1/np.sqrt(2)
tau_ghz = three_tangle(ghz)
print(f"GHZ state: tau = {tau_ghz:.4f}, class = {classify_state(ghz)}")

# W state
w = np.zeros(8, dtype=complex)
w[1] = w[2] = w[4] = 1/np.sqrt(3)
tau_w = three_tangle(w)
print(f"W state: tau = {tau_w:.4f}, class = {classify_state(w)}")

# Product state
product = np.zeros(8, dtype=complex)
product[0] = 1
tau_prod = three_tangle(product)
print(f"Product |000>: tau = {tau_prod:.4f}, class = {classify_state(product)}")

# General GHZ-type: a|000> + b|111>
alpha, beta = 0.8, 0.6
general_ghz = np.zeros(8, dtype=complex)
general_ghz[0] = alpha
general_ghz[7] = beta
general_ghz /= np.linalg.norm(general_ghz)
tau_gen = three_tangle(general_ghz)
print(f"General GHZ-type: tau = {tau_gen:.4f}, class = {classify_state(general_ghz)}")
exercises.solution:
import numpy as np

def three_tangle(state):
    """
    Calculate the three-tangle for a three-qubit state.
    
    Uses the Cayley hyperdeterminant formula.
    """
    # Ensure normalized
    state = state / np.linalg.norm(state)
    
    # Reshape to 2x2x2 tensor
    # Indices: a[i,j,k] corresponds to |ijk>
    a = state.reshape(2, 2, 2)
    
    # Extract coefficients
    # Index mapping: |000>=a[0,0,0], |001>=a[0,0,1], ..., |111>=a[1,1,1]
    a000 = a[0, 0, 0]
    a001 = a[0, 0, 1]
    a010 = a[0, 1, 0]
    a011 = a[0, 1, 1]
    a100 = a[1, 0, 0]
    a101 = a[1, 0, 1]
    a110 = a[1, 1, 0]
    a111 = a[1, 1, 1]
    
    # Cayley hyperdeterminant formula for 2x2x2 tensor
    # D = a000^2 * a111^2 + a001^2 * a110^2 + a010^2 * a101^2 + a011^2 * a100^2
    #   - 2*(a000*a111*a011*a100 + a000*a111*a101*a010 + a000*a111*a001*a110
    #       + a011*a100*a101*a010 + a011*a100*a001*a110 + a101*a010*a001*a110)
    #   + 4*(a000*a011*a101*a110 + a001*a010*a100*a111)
    
    term1 = (a000**2 * a111**2 + a001**2 * a110**2 + 
             a010**2 * a101**2 + a011**2 * a100**2)
    
    term2 = -2 * (a000*a111*a011*a100 + a000*a111*a101*a010 + a000*a111*a001*a110 +
                  a011*a100*a101*a010 + a011*a100*a001*a110 + a101*a010*a001*a110)
    
    term3 = 4 * (a000*a011*a101*a110 + a001*a010*a100*a111)
    
    hyperdeterminant = term1 + term2 + term3
    
    # Three-tangle is 4 times the absolute value squared of hyperdeterminant
    tau = 4 * np.abs(hyperdeterminant)
    
    return np.real(tau)

def classify_state(state):
    """
    Classify a three-qubit state based on three-tangle.
    """
    tau = three_tangle(state)
    
    # Also check for product/biseparable by looking at reduced density matrices
    rho = np.outer(state, state.conj())
    
    # Check bipartite entanglement across different cuts
    def bipartite_entropy(rho, n_a):
        dim_a = 2**n_a
        dim_b = 8 // dim_a
        rho_reshaped = rho.reshape(dim_a, dim_b, dim_a, dim_b)
        rho_a = np.trace(rho_reshaped, axis1=1, axis2=3)
        eigs = np.linalg.eigvalsh(rho_a)
        eigs = np.maximum(eigs, 1e-15)
        return -np.sum(eigs * np.log2(eigs))
    
    S_A = bipartite_entropy(rho, 1)  # A|BC cut
    S_B = bipartite_entropy(rho, 1)  # B|AC cut (similar structure)
    
    if tau > 0.01:
        return "GHZ-class"
    elif S_A > 0.01:  # Has bipartite entanglement
        return "W-class"
    else:
        return "Product/Biseparable"

# Test classification
print("SLOCC Classification using Three-Tangle:")
print("=" * 50)

# GHZ state
ghz = np.zeros(8, dtype=complex)
ghz[0] = ghz[7] = 1/np.sqrt(2)
tau_ghz = three_tangle(ghz)
print(f"\nGHZ state: (|000> + |111>)/sqrt(2)")
print(f"  Three-tangle tau = {tau_ghz:.4f}")
print(f"  Classification: {classify_state(ghz)}")

# W state
w = np.zeros(8, dtype=complex)
w[1] = w[2] = w[4] = 1/np.sqrt(3)
tau_w = three_tangle(w)
print(f"\nW state: (|001> + |010> + |100>)/sqrt(3)")
print(f"  Three-tangle tau = {tau_w:.4f}")
print(f"  Classification: {classify_state(w)}")

# Product state
product = np.zeros(8, dtype=complex)
product[0] = 1
tau_prod = three_tangle(product)
print(f"\nProduct state |000>:")
print(f"  Three-tangle tau = {tau_prod:.4f}")
print(f"  Classification: {classify_state(product)}")

# General GHZ-type: a|000> + b|111>
alpha, beta = 0.8, 0.6
general_ghz = np.zeros(8, dtype=complex)
general_ghz[0] = alpha
general_ghz[7] = beta
general_ghz /= np.linalg.norm(general_ghz)
tau_gen = three_tangle(general_ghz)
print(f"\nGeneral GHZ-type ({alpha:.1f}|000> + {beta:.1f}|111>):")
print(f"  Three-tangle tau = {tau_gen:.4f}")
print(f"  Classification: {classify_state(general_ghz)}")

print("\n" + "=" * 50)
print("Key insight: tau > 0 iff state has GHZ-type entanglement")
print("W state has tau = 0 but is still genuinely entangled")
hints: Reshape the 8-element state vector to a 2x2x2 tensor
The hyperdeterminant formula has three groups of terms
For GHZ states, tau = 1; for W states, tau = 0
Consider using symbolic indices: a000 = a[0,0,0], etc.

exercises.title: Genuine Multipartite Entanglement Detection
exercises.description: Implement a function to detect genuine multipartite entanglement (GME) using entanglement witnesses and bipartition analysis. Check entanglement across all possible bipartitions and determine if the state is genuinely n-partite entangled.
exercises.starterCode:
import numpy as np
from itertools import combinations

def check_all_bipartitions(state, n):
    """
    Check entanglement across all possible bipartitions.
    
    For n qubits, there are 2^(n-1) - 1 non-trivial bipartitions.
    
    Args:
        state: state vector of dimension 2^n
        n: number of qubits
    
    Returns:
        dict with results for each bipartition
    """
    # Your code here
    pass

def is_genuinely_entangled(state, n):
    """
    Determine if a state has genuine multipartite entanglement.
    
    A state is GME if it is entangled across ALL bipartitions.
    
    Args:
        state: state vector
        n: number of qubits
    
    Returns:
        dict with:
        - 'is_gme': boolean
        - 'bipartition_results': detailed results
        - 'weakest_bipartition': bipartition with smallest entanglement
    """
    # Your code here
    pass

# Test on various states
print("GME Detection Analysis:")
print("=" * 50)

# GHZ state (should be GME)
ghz = np.zeros(8, dtype=complex)
ghz[0] = ghz[7] = 1/np.sqrt(2)
result_ghz = is_genuinely_entangled(ghz, 3)
print(f"GHZ state: GME = {result_ghz['is_gme']}")

# W state (should be GME)
w = np.zeros(8, dtype=complex)
w[1] = w[2] = w[4] = 1/np.sqrt(3)
result_w = is_genuinely_entangled(w, 3)
print(f"W state: GME = {result_w['is_gme']}")

# Biseparable state |0> x |Bell> (should NOT be GME)
bell = np.array([1, 0, 0, 1]) / np.sqrt(2)
bisep = np.kron(np.array([1, 0]), bell)  # |0> x |Phi+>
result_bisep = is_genuinely_entangled(bisep, 3)
print(f"Biseparable |0>|Bell>: GME = {result_bisep['is_gme']}")

# Product state (should NOT be GME)
product = np.zeros(8)
product[0] = 1
result_prod = is_genuinely_entangled(product, 3)
print(f"Product |000>: GME = {result_prod['is_gme']}")
exercises.solution:
import numpy as np
from itertools import combinations

def partial_transpose(rho, dim_a, dim_b):
    """Compute partial transpose."""
    rho_tensor = rho.reshape(dim_a, dim_b, dim_a, dim_b)
    rho_pt = np.transpose(rho_tensor, (0, 3, 2, 1)).reshape(dim_a * dim_b, dim_a * dim_b)
    return rho_pt

def negativity(rho, dim_a, dim_b):
    """Compute negativity from partial transpose."""
    rho_pt = partial_transpose(rho, dim_a, dim_b)
    eigenvalues = np.linalg.eigvalsh(rho_pt)
    neg_eigs = eigenvalues[eigenvalues < -1e-10]
    return np.sum(np.abs(neg_eigs))

def check_all_bipartitions(state, n):
    """
    Check entanglement across all possible bipartitions.
    """
    dim = 2**n
    rho = np.outer(state, state.conj())
    results = {}
    
    # Generate all non-trivial bipartitions
    # Bipartition: subset S vs complement S_bar
    # We only need subsets of size 1 to n//2 to avoid duplicates
    
    for k in range(1, (n // 2) + 1):
        for subset in combinations(range(n), k):
            # Skip if this is more than half (already covered by complement)
            if k == n // 2 and subset[0] != 0:
                continue
            
            complement = tuple(i for i in range(n) if i not in subset)
            partition_name = f"{subset}|{complement}"
            
            # Reorder qubits so partition is contiguous
            # For simplicity, compute reduced density matrix for subset
            # and check its purity
            
            # Dimensions
            dim_a = 2**len(subset)
            dim_b = 2**len(complement)
            
            # For bipartite negativity, we need to reorganize the state
            # This is complex; we'll use a simpler approach via reduced entropy
            
            # Compute reduced density matrix for subset
            rho_tensor = rho.reshape([2]*n + [2]*n)
            
            # Trace over complement qubits
            axes_to_trace = [(c, c + n) for c in complement]
            rho_reduced = rho.copy()
            
            # Simplified: check entanglement using linear entropy
            # For pure states, if S(rho_A) > 0, the cut is entangled
            
            # Permute and reshape for this bipartition
            perm = list(subset) + list(complement)
            state_reordered = state.reshape([2]*n)
            state_reordered = np.transpose(state_reordered, perm)
            state_reordered = state_reordered.reshape(dim_a * dim_b)
            
            rho_reordered = np.outer(state_reordered, state_reordered.conj())
            neg = negativity(rho_reordered, dim_a, dim_b)
            
            results[partition_name] = {
                'subset': subset,
                'complement': complement,
                'negativity': neg,
                'is_entangled': neg > 1e-10
            }
    
    return results

def is_genuinely_entangled(state, n):
    """
    Determine if a state has genuine multipartite entanglement.
    """
    bipartition_results = check_all_bipartitions(state, n)
    
    # GME requires entanglement across ALL bipartitions
    all_entangled = all(r['is_entangled'] for r in bipartition_results.values())
    
    # Find weakest bipartition
    min_neg = float('inf')
    weakest = None
    for name, result in bipartition_results.items():
        if result['negativity'] < min_neg:
            min_neg = result['negativity']
            weakest = name
    
    return {
        'is_gme': all_entangled,
        'bipartition_results': bipartition_results,
        'weakest_bipartition': weakest,
        'weakest_negativity': min_neg
    }

# Test on various states
print("GME Detection Analysis:")
print("=" * 50)

# GHZ state
print("\n1. GHZ state: (|000> + |111>)/sqrt(2)")
ghz = np.zeros(8, dtype=complex)
ghz[0] = ghz[7] = 1/np.sqrt(2)
result_ghz = is_genuinely_entangled(ghz, 3)
print(f"   GME: {result_ghz['is_gme']}")
print(f"   Weakest bipartition: {result_ghz['weakest_bipartition']}")
print(f"   Weakest negativity: {result_ghz['weakest_negativity']:.4f}")

# W state
print("\n2. W state: (|001> + |010> + |100>)/sqrt(3)")
w = np.zeros(8, dtype=complex)
w[1] = w[2] = w[4] = 1/np.sqrt(3)
result_w = is_genuinely_entangled(w, 3)
print(f"   GME: {result_w['is_gme']}")
print(f"   Weakest bipartition: {result_w['weakest_bipartition']}")

# Biseparable state |0> x |Bell>
print("\n3. Biseparable state: |0> tensor |Bell>")
bell = np.array([1, 0, 0, 1]) / np.sqrt(2)
bisep = np.kron(np.array([1, 0]), bell)
result_bisep = is_genuinely_entangled(bisep, 3)
print(f"   GME: {result_bisep['is_gme']}")
for name, res in result_bisep['bipartition_results'].items():
    print(f"   {name}: entangled = {res['is_entangled']}, neg = {res['negativity']:.4f}")

# Product state
print("\n4. Product state: |000>")
product = np.zeros(8)
product[0] = 1
result_prod = is_genuinely_entangled(product, 3)
print(f"   GME: {result_prod['is_gme']}")

print("\n" + "=" * 50)
print("Summary:")
print("- GHZ and W states are genuinely multipartite entangled")
print("- Biseparable states fail at least one bipartition test")
print("- Product states fail all bipartition tests")
hints: For each bipartition, reorder qubits and compute negativity
GME requires entanglement across ALL bipartitions
A biseparable state will have at least one bipartition with zero negativity

testCases.expected: is_gme = False, at least one bipartition separable
